questions.scenario:
A placement review for devpolaris-orders-api shows:

orders-alb: internet-facing
orders-api tasks: should receive traffic only from ALB
orders-db: should receive traffic only from app tasks

Which subnet plan best matches that intent?

options.text: ALB in public subnets, ECS tasks in private app subnets, RDS in private database subnets.
ALB, ECS tasks, and RDS all in public subnets so each layer can be tested from a browser.
ALB in private subnets and ECS tasks in public subnets so the app can receive internet traffic first.
RDS in public subnets and ALB in database subnets so the database owns the public entry point.

questions.explanation: The public edge belongs in public subnets, while the app and database stay private behind intended callers. That separates the browser entry point from runtime and data storage. It is a clean first production shape for this service.

questions.scenario:
The subnet named `orders-public-a` has this route table:

Destination      Target
10.20.0.0/16     local

No internet gateway route is associated with it. What should the team conclude?

options.text: The name says public, but the route table does not prove public internet-bound routing.
The subnet is public because any subnet with `public` in the name gets an internet gateway route automatically.
The subnet is public because the local route is the same thing as an internet gateway route.
The subnet is private only if every resource inside it has no security group.

questions.explanation: Public and private behavior comes from routing, not labels. Without a route to an internet gateway, the subnet name alone does not make internet-bound traffic public. Names help humans, but route tables decide behavior.

questions.scenario: Which facts are needed before a resource in an IPv4 subnet is normally reachable from the public internet?

options.text: A route table path to an internet gateway for internet-bound traffic.
A public IPv4 address on the resource or a public load balancer address in front of it.
Security rules that allow the intended protocol and port.
A private subnet name that includes the word `public` for the AWS console.

questions.explanation: Public reachability usually needs routing, an internet-routable address or public load balancer, and allowed traffic rules. Missing any one of those can break the path. A name is useful documentation, not a network control.

questions.scenario: A task definition sets `assignPublicIp: ENABLED` for orders-api tasks. The desired design says only the ALB should receive customer traffic. What is the best review direction?

options.text: Use private app subnets with public IP assignment disabled, then allow task inbound only from the ALB security group.
Keep public task IPs and rely on the database security group to hide the app port.
Move the ALB to private database subnets so customers must use task public IPs.
Keep public task IPs because ECS tasks cannot receive ALB traffic without them.

questions.explanation: The app tasks do not need direct public addresses when the ALB is the entry point. Private placement plus a task SG source of the ALB SG keeps the path narrow. The ALB can target private task IPs.

questions.scenario: The team plans NAT gateways for devpolaris-orders-api. Where should the NAT gateways live and why?

options.text: In public subnets, because NAT needs its own path to the internet gateway while serving private subnet egress.
In private database subnets, because databases should own all outbound internet traffic.
In the same private app subnet as the ECS tasks, because NAT makes that subnet public.
In Route 53, because NAT translates public hostnames for browser requests.

questions.explanation: A NAT gateway helps private resources start outbound connections, and it needs a public subnet path to the internet gateway. It does not make the private app subnet a public entry point. The ALB remains the intended inbound path.
